elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs) possess extensive knowledge and exhibit promising reasoning abilities, however, they still struggle to perform well in complex, dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-002: Real-world tasks require handling intricate interactions, advanced spatial reasoning, long-term planning, and continuous exploration of new strategies—areas in which we lack effective methodologies for comprehensively evaluating these capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this gap, we introduce \our{ , a novel benchmark designed to assess the agentic capabilities of LLMs and VLMs through a diverse set of challenging games.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Our benchmark incorporates a range of existing reinforcement learning environments with varying levels of difficulty, including tasks that are solvable by non-expert humans in seconds to extremely challenging ones that may take years to master (e.g., the NetHack Learning Environment).
elements.sent-2411-005: We devise fine-grained metrics to measure performance and conduct an extensive evaluation of several popular open-source and closed-source LLMs and VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our findings indicate that while current models achieve partial success in the easier games, they struggle significantly with more challenging tasks.
elements.sent-2411-007: Notably, we observe severe deficiencies in vision-based decision-making, as models perform worse when visual representations of the environments are provided.
elements.sent-2411-008: We release \our{ as an open and user-friendly benchmark to facilitate future research and development in the agentic community.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Recent successes of Large Language Models (LLMs) have renewed interest in building general-purpose agents capable of autonomously achieving complex goals~\cite{yang2023auto .}
elements.sent-2411-010: LLMs possess vast knowledge across domains~\citep{brown2020language,hendrycks2020measuring , can reason in specific scenarios~\citep{wei2022emergent,shinn2023reflexion,rein2023gpqa , and can reliably follow human instructions in simple settings~\citep{ouyang2022training .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: These abilities suggest that LLMs have the potential to become efficient agents, capable of autonomously performing a wide range of human tasks that require sequential decision making.
elements.sent-2411-012: In the present day, however, state-of-the-art models continue to exhibit persistent failure modes on many of the skills that are crucial for autonomous real-world interaction.
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, LLMs fail to act robustly in dynamic environments, and they cannot reliably learn from mistakes, reason about space and time, or plan over long time horizons~\citep{xing2024understanding,yamada2023evaluating,kambhampati2024llms .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Improving our understanding of LLM capabilities through rigorous, safe evaluations is key for assessing the risks and limitations of deploying agentic LLMs in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-015: Current agentic benchmarks evaluate LLM performance in settings that involve no more than a few dozen rounds of interaction between a model and an environment, e.g., solving simple office tasks~\citep{wang2024officebench , navigating the Internet ~\citep{zhou2023webarena , and resolving GitHub issues~\citep{jimenez2023swe .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: New agentic prompting frameworks and improvements to short-horizon reasoning via LLMs like OpenAI o1 have led to dramatic and fast-paced gains in state-of-the-art performance on these benchmarks \citep{openai2024o1, wang2023voyager, fernando2023promptbreeder, hu2024automated .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, many realistic tasks require orders of magnitude more interactions~\citep{pignatiello2020decision,wansink2007mindless .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{images/diagram_v15.png} \caption{\textbf{An overview of the \our{} Benchmark for evaluating LLMs on long-context interactive tasks}. Submissions of new inference-time methods for improving the capabilities of an existing model via an ``agentic strategy'' need only modify the \texttt{ {agent.py}} file. Similarly, benchmarking a new model zero-shot can be done by adjusting a configuration file in \texttt{ {client.py}}. The agent class includes a prompt builder to manage observation history, and a client that abstracts the complexities of various APIs and model-serving frameworks. The \texttt{ {env\_wrapper.py}} file standardizes interaction across settings, and the evaluator executes agents and collects performance metrics. } \label{fig:diagram}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we argue that the next frontier for language and vision-language model capabilities lies in long-horizon reasoning and decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-019: To that end, we propose~\our{ : Benchmarking Agentic LLM/VLM Reasoning On Games.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Games have historically served as highly effective metrics for evaluating progress in deep reinforcement learning research~\citep{bellemare2013arcade, silver2018general, schrittwieser2020mastering, vinyals2019grandmaster .}
elements.sent-2411-021: By aggregating many different game environments into a single evaluation, we look to spur progress on developing truly generalist agents that can meaningfully address embodied, real world tasks.
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically \our{ enables seamless running of LLM and VLM agents on BabyAI, Crafter, TextWorld, Baba Is AI, MiniHack, and NetHack~\citep{babyai_iclr19, hafner2021benchmarking, cote2019textworld, cloos2024baba, samvelyan2021minihack, kuttler2020nethack .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: These environments have lightweight simulators, ensuring that the benchmark is affordable for the research community.
elements.sent-2411-024: Furthermore, while all of these games are long-horizon, they span a broad range of difficulty levels, from tasks where we see fair zero-shot performance by state-of-the-art long-context models (BabyAI) to those where even specialized neural models trained on billions of in-domain datapoints make very limited progress (NetHack)~\citep{piterbarg2023diff, klissarov2023motif, wolczyk2024fine .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Using the six proposed environments, we evaluate the capabilities of various popular LLMs and VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-026: We employ a fine-grained metric that captures how close each model is to completing a task, which gives us a thorough understanding of the resulting trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-027: In our qualitative analysis, we study the agents' capabilities for spatial reasoning, systematic exploration, long-term planning, and discovering environment dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-028: We find that the current top LLMs show promise on the simplest tasks but completely fail to make meaningful progress on the more difficult tasks, such as MiniHack and NetHack.
elements.sent-2411-029: Some of the models exhibit knowledge about the game from pre-training but fail to use it in practice.
elements.sent-2411-030: For example, in NetHack, GPT-4o often dies from the consumption of rotten food, even though, when prompted, it correctly identifies it as very dangerous.
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, we study the impact of the input representation.
elements.sent-2411-032: Although the majority of the environments were created with vision in mind, we find that multimodal LLMs perform much worse when also presented with an image of the environment rather than a textual-only description of the observation.
elements.sent-2411-033: This suggests that reliable vision-based decision-making is currently far outside our reach.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our results show that \our{ is a very difficult benchmark that still allows us to observe fine-grained progress in crucial areas such as long-term planning, spatial reasoning and navigation.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We share the codebase and open the benchmark for external submissions.
elements.sent-2411-036: We summarize our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We provide a fine-grained metric for model evaluation, and we develop a novel data-informed progression system for NetHack.
elements.sent-2411-038: Baseline evaluations of state-of-the-art LLMs on \our{ using zero-shot prompting, in both Language-Vision and Language-only modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-039: We show that while models exhibit decent performance on easier games, all are very far from solving the hardest game in the benchmark, NetHack.
elements.sent-2411-040: We observe that the performance drops further when images of the environment are presented, suggesting severe problems with VLM decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-041: We perform a qualitative analysis of the results across capabilities such as spatial reasoning, systematic exploration, and long-term planning.
elements.sent-2411-042: We identify an intriguing knowing-doing gap where the models cannot employ the knowledge they possess.
elements.sent-2411-043: An open-source toolkit for benchmarking long-context models on \our{ .}
elements.sent-2411-044: This toolkit enables researchers and practitioners to quickly evaluate model performance.
elements.sent-2411-045: While the baseline evaluations performed in this paper are zero-shot, the \our{ toolkit supports inference-time prompting strategies like chain-of-thought \citep{wei2022chain , few-shot learning, and more.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: It supports model evaluation on challenging reinforcement learning environments that test skills such as long-term planning, spatial reasoning, and the ability to deduce the mechanics of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-047: By design, the \our{ framework explicitly decouples inference-time prompting strategies from underlying models.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The goal of this design choice is two-fold: (1) to facilitate rapid prototyping of inference-time methods for improving model performance on long-context decision-making beyond zero-shot prompting and (2) to ensure that model evaluations are consistent and rigorous.
elements.sent-2411-049: In the remainder of this section, we introduce the game environments evaluated in the benchmark and we discuss our protocols for model submission to the \our{ Benchmark Leaderboard\footnote{This Leaderboard will open to the public at the time of publication.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: BabyAI.~\citep{babyai_iclr19, carta2023grounding A simple, two-dimensional grid-world in which the agent has to solve tasks of varying complexity described in natural language (e.g., ``go to the blue ball, then pick up the grey key'').}
elements.sent-2411-051: Agents are tested across five different types of navigation tasks, see Appendix \ref{babyai .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Crafter.~\citep{hafner2021benchmarking A Minecraft-inspired grid environment where the player has to explore, gather resources and craft items to ensure their survival.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Agents are evaluated based on the number of achieved milestones, such as discovering new resources and crafting tools, see Appendix~\ref{crafter .}
elements.sent-2411-054: TextWorld.~\citep{cote2019textworld An entirely text-based game with no visual component, where the agent has to explore mazes and interact with everyday objects through natural language (e.g., ``cook potato with oven'').}
elements.sent-2411-055: Unlike the other environments in \our{ , TextWorld is not a grid-world.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Models are evaluated on three different tasks, see Appendix \ref{textworld .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Baba Is AI.~\citep{cloos2024baba An environment based on the popular puzzle video game Baba Is You.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The player manipulates the rules of the game world by pushing word blocks, altering how objects interact.
elements.sent-2411-059: Agents are tested on 40 puzzles, see Appendix~\ref{babaisai .}
elements.sent-2411-060: MiniHack.~\citep{samvelyan2021minihack MiniHack is a multi-task framework built on top of the NetHack Learning Environment~\citep{kuttler2020nethack .}}

elements.sent-2411-062: Collectively, they assess a wide range of skills, including exploration, navigation, long-term planning, and resource management, see Appendix~\ref{minihack .}

elements.sent-2411-070: This diverse set of environments positions \our{ as a comprehensive benchmark for assessing the capabilities of LLM agents, making it a valuable tool for evaluating their performance for years to come.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The \our{ benchmark accepts two types of submissions.. New Models.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Submissions may include any type of new model, such as large language models (LLMs), vision-language models (VLMs), large-action models (LAMs), or fine-tuned versions of existing models.
elements.sent-2411-073: The key requirement is that these models must be capable of generating actions in natural language.
elements.sent-2411-074: By default, these models will be evaluated zero-shot.. Agentic Strategies.
elements.sent-2411-075: Submissions may propose novel inference-time prompting strategies for improving the reasoning, planning, or in-context learning capability of an existing model.
elements.sent-2411-076: These strategies should extend beyond simple zero-shot prompting for direct action prediction, demonstrating more sophisticated techniques for inference-time decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-077: In this section, we provide a description of our protocols for evaluating state-of-the-art, long-context LLMs and VLMs on \our{ .}
elements.sent-2411-078: These evaluations are intended to serve as baselines for the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-079: As a result, they probe zero-shot performance only.
elements.sent-2411-080: We aim to keep the evaluation setting simple.
elements.sent-2411-081: During each timestep of interaction, agents are prompted to output the next action as a natural language string, conditioned on their past interaction history in the environment.
elements.sent-2411-082: To perform successfully in \our{ , models must demonstrate robust instruction-following capabilities, including reading and interpreting game rules, understanding the action space, and producing valid actions to complete tasks effectively.}
elements.sent-2411-083: To address cases where the LLMs/VLMs output hallucinated or invalid actions, \our{ provides feedback to the agent indicating the action’s invalidity, it then executes a default fallback action (such as a “do-nothing” action or a standard move like “north”), and logs the occurrence for trajectory statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-084: This ensures that the interaction remains continuous and robust while enabling users to analyze the context and frequency of such errors in post-evaluation analysis.
elements.sent-2411-085: A diagrammatic visualization of \our{ is shown in Figure \ref{fig:diagram .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: We conceptualize the agent as a combination of the underlying LLM/VLM model and a particular prompting strategy.
elements.sent-2411-087: We provide a unified client wrapper that seamlessly integrates APIs for closed-source LLMs and VLMs such as OpenAI, Gemini, and Claude and allows users to effortlessly switch and evaluate models.
elements.sent-2411-088: For the evaluation of locally-served models, we include native support for the vLLM library~\citep{kwon2023efficient , which optimizes throughput by efficiently batching generation requests.}
elements.sent-2411-089: We use multiple seeds for each environment to ensure the statistical significance of the results.
elements.sent-2411-090: Metrics To ensure a fair and interpretable evaluation, we introduce a standardized metric, scoring performance on each task within a range of 0 to 100.
elements.sent-2411-091: For environments like MiniHack, BabyAI, and Baba Is AI, each episode is scored as either 0 or 100 based on task completion.
elements.sent-2411-092: For TextWorld, Crafter, and NetHack we use as the score a real number between 0 and 100, representing the proportion of achievements toward the maximum score.
elements.sent-2411-093: For NetHack, as the game scoring system does not adequately reflect actual progression \citep{wolczyk2024fine , we propose a novel, data-informed progression metric, described in Appendix \ref{NetHack-progression-metric , to better capture agent performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Performance \our{ supports highly parallelized evaluations, leveraging the lightweight simulators of each of the environments in the suite.}
elements.sent-2411-095: These evaluations allow multiple agents and environment instances to run concurrently with minimal computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-096: Environment instances run asynchronously from one another, accommodating varying observation lengths and ensuring that agents with faster generation speeds (per action) are not affected by slower agent bottlenecks.

elements.sent-2411-126: We conducted an analysis of the model trajectories across the environments to identify common behaviors and challenges specific to each setting.
elements.sent-2411-127: Spatial Reasoning While language models demonstrate some proficiency in basic navigation, they exhibit significant limitations in more complex spatial reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-128: In the BabyAI suite, we observed significant shortcomings in the agents' ability to place objects adjacent to other objects, which is required in some scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-129: In NetHack and MiniHack CorridorBattle, good spatial reasoning is crucial during combat, as players need to maneuver within confined corridors to avoid being surrounded by monsters.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, the agents frequently ended up cornered.
elements.sent-2411-131: Systematic Exploration Our experiments revealed a significant weakness in the models' ability to explore.
elements.sent-2411-132: In TextWorld's Coin Collector, where agents must explore a house to locate a coin, agents often wander aimlessly, revisiting rooms they've already explored while missing important areas entirely.
elements.sent-2411-133: An efficient agent would behave in DFS-like manner, methodically searching each room, keeping track of visited areas and prioritizing unexplored spaces.
elements.sent-2411-134: The more complex quests in MiniHack expose similar issues, with models failing to efficiently navigate maze-like structures.
elements.sent-2411-135: Long-term planning The agents exhibit substantial deficiencies in devising and executing long-term plans.
elements.sent-2411-136: We observe near-zero performance on MiniHack, and NLE, which both require careful planning.
elements.sent-2411-137: In particular, we do not observe a single successful trajectory in the Boxoban logical puzzles in MiniHack, which requires careful planning at every step in order to avoid irreversible failures.
elements.sent-2411-138: LLMs, with the finite amount of compute available to them in a single forward pass, are necessarily confined to solving some subset of reasoning problems.
elements.sent-2411-139: We observe that with the current models' depth, number of flops, and reasoning solution templates embedded in the weights, these models cannot solve the reasoning tasks in \our{ .}
elements.sent-2411-140: We see a notable improvement with OpenAI o1's chain of thought capabilities on NetHack, performing close to three times better than its closest competitor in language-only mode Claude-3.5-Sonnet.
elements.sent-2411-141: However, its average progression of 1.57\% is still far from satisfactory.
elements.sent-2411-142: Discovering and Leveraging Environment Dynamics Some games require inferring non-trivial causal structure through experimentation to come up with new strategies.
elements.sent-2411-143: For example, a player might identify a {potion of paralysis by drinking it, and then realize they can use this strategically by throwing such potions at enemies to incapacitate them.}
elements.sent-2411-144: This kind of experimentation and strategic thinking is crucial for success in NetHack.
elements.sent-2411-145: However, current models struggle to formulate and execute such context-dependent strategies.
elements.sent-2411-146: In MiniHack Quests environments, models fail to devise and implement multi-step strategies, such as utilizing {wand of cold or {ring of levitation to cross lava rivers.}}
elements.sent-2411-147: In Crafter, where agents can handle basic tasks such as collecting wood, crafting items, drinking water, and even engaging in combat, they fail to learn long-term survival skills such as building shelters for protection against nocturnal threats.
elements.sent-2411-148: Knowing-Doing Gap We observe a pronounced ``knowing-doing'' gap, where models execute undesirable actions during gameplay despite knowledge of their negative consequences.
elements.sent-2411-149: For instance, in NetHack, models often exit the dungeon shortly after starting the game, resulting in an instant game termination.
elements.sent-2411-150: When queried in a separate thread about the consequences of exiting the first level in NetHack, they correctly identify that it results in an instant death, making it is a highly undesirable action.
elements.sent-2411-151: Similarly, although the models correctly identify that eating rotten food in NetHack can result in death, this remains a common cause of failure, underscoring a disconnect between knowledge and decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-152: Additionally, models tend to ignore even the hints directly present in the input prompt and die from overeating even when advised against it.
elements.sent-2411-153: To study this problem in more detail, we prepared a questionnaire probing basic NetHack knowledge (see Appendix~\ref{nle_questionare_table ).}

elements.paragraph-2411-004: Computational Limitations of Large Language Models
elements.sent-2411-202: Mechanistic interpretability could provide valuable insights for understanding the computational limitations of agentic LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-203: The computational expressiveness of LLMs is fundamentally linked with the ability to solve complex reasoning problems ~\citep{wei2022emergent .}
elements.sent-2411-204: While current models perform well on simple tasks such as navigation and object manipulation, they struggle with more complex tasks that could require non-trivial and general-purpose computation, for example, building a shelter or developing combat strategies.

ewline Tips:
ewline - Examine the level carefully, noting all objects and text blocks present.
ewline - Identify the current rules, which are formed by text blocks in the format "[Subject] IS [Property]" (e.g. "BABA IS YOU").
ewline - Consider how you can change or create new rules by moving text blocks around.
ewline - Remember that you can only move objects or text that are not defined as "STOP" or similar immovable properties.
ewline - Your goal is usually to reach an object defined as "WIN", but this can be changed.
ewline - Think creatively about how changing rules can alter the properties and behaviors of objects in unexpected ways.
ewline - If stuck, try breaking apart existing rules or forming completely new ones.
ewline - Sometimes the solution involves making yourself a different object or changing what counts as the win condition.

elements.sent-2411-300: The framework allows users to define environments using a human-readable description language or a simple Python interface, giving fine-grained control over environment elements such as terrain, objects, monsters, and traps.
elements.sent-2411-301: MiniHack offers a diverse array of tasks, which can be broadly categorized into three main groups: Navigation Tasks, Skill Acquisition Tasks, and Ported Tasks.
elements.sent-2411-302: To enable interaction with language models, we use NetHack Language Wrapper described in the NetHack Appendix~\ref{nethack_language_wrapper .}
elements.sent-2411-303: From the MiniHack Navigation Tasks, we picked Maze 9x9, Maze 15x15, Corridor and CorridorBattle, which challenge the agent to reach the goal position by overcoming various difficulties on their way, such as fighting monsters in corridors and navigating through complex or procedurally generated mazes.
elements.sent-2411-304: These tasks feature a relatively small action space, i.e., movement towards 8 compass directions, and based on the environment, search, kick, open, and eat actions.
elements.sent-2411-305: From the MiniHack Skill Acquisition Tasks, we picked Quest (with three different difficulty levels, Easy, Medium, and Hard), which challenges the agent to use objects found in the environment to cross a lava river (these objects can provide levitation or freezing abilities), fight monsters, navigate through rooms or mazes and towards the end of the quest use a wand of death to defeat a powerful monster guarding the goal location.
elements.sent-2411-306: We additionally test the agents on MiniHack Boxoban.
elements.sent-2411-307: This family of environments is an adaptation of the Boxoban puzzle game, which itself is inspired by the classic Sokoban.
elements.sent-2411-308: These environments present a challenging puzzle-solving task within the MiniHack framework, leveraging the NetHack game mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-309: The primary goal in MiniHack Boxoban is to push four boulders (MiniHack's equivalent of boxes) onto four designated goal locations, which are represented by fountains.
elements.sent-2411-310: This task requires strategic thinking and planning, as the agent must carefully maneuver the boulders through the environment without getting them stuck in corners or against walls.
elements.sent-2411-311: We provide MiniHack results for LLM and VLM mode in Tables~\ref{minihack_appendix_table1 and~\ref{minihack_appendix_table2 , standard errors were computed using 5 seeds for each task.}}

elements.sent-2411-315: The NetHack Learning Environment (NLE)~\citep{kuttler2020nethack is a scalable, procedurally generated, stochastic, rich, and challenging environment designed to drive long-term research in RL on problems such as exploration, planning, skill acquisition, and language-conditioned RL.}
elements.sent-2411-316: Built around the classic and highly complex terminal roguelike game NetHack, NLE provides a complex and dynamic environment where agents must navigate through procedurally generated dungeons, interact with hundreds of entity types, and learn to overcome various challenges.
elements.sent-2411-317: The goal of the player is to descend through procedurally generated dungeon levels while killing monsters, solving puzzles, and gathering better equipment in order to retrieve the Amulet of Yendor and finally ascend back to the surface to win the game.
elements.sent-2411-318: NetHack is notoriously challenging, even for human players.
elements.sent-2411-319: Mastering the game can take years even with online resources like the NetHack Wiki.
elements.sent-2411-320: Success in NetHack demands long-term strategic planning, as a winning game can involve hundreds of thousands of steps, as well as short-term tactics to fight hordes of monsters.
elements.sent-2411-321: NetHack has already been used extensively as a testbed for RL agents~\citep{wolczyk2024fine, piterbarg2023diff, hambro2022dungeons ; tabula-rasa RL agents particularly struggle due to sparse reward, complex credit assignment, extremely long-time-horizon, and high stochasticity of the game.}
elements.sent-2411-322: The current state-of-the-art agent still remains a hand-coded symbolic policy \citep{hambro2022insights .}
elements.sent-2411-323: The NetHack Language Wrapper \citep{goodger2023nethack is a tool designed to interface with the NLE and MiniHack by translating non-language observations into text-based representations.}
elements.sent-2411-324: This wrapper, converts various NLE observations such as \verb|glyphs|, \verb|blstats|, \verb|tty_chars|, \verb|inv_letters|, \verb|inv_strs|, and \verb|tty_cursor| into readable text equivalents.
elements.sent-2411-325: For example, it transforms the visual display of the game environment into a textual description, including details about the surroundings, inventory, and player statistics.
elements.sent-2411-326: The wrapper also supports text-based actions, allowing users to interact with the environment using commands like \verb|wait|, \verb|apply|, and \verb|north|, which are then converted into the discrete actions required by the NLE.
elements.sent-2411-327: This functionality enables easier interaction with the NetHack environment, particularly for language models.
elements.figure-2411-017: \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/dungeon_levels.png} \caption{NetHack progression by dungeon level reached} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/experience_levels.png} \caption{NetHack progression by experience level} \end{subfigure} \caption{} \label{NLE_progression}
elements.sent-2411-328: NetHack features an in-game scoring system that rewards players for actions such as killing monsters, identifying objects, eating food, collecting gold and items, and ultimately ascending in the game.
elements.sent-2411-329: However, we argue that this scoring system does not effectively capture true game progression, as players can win the game with scores ranging from a few hundred thousand to several million points.
elements.sent-2411-330: To address this limitation, we developed a novel, data-informed progression metric using a dataset of human-played NetHack games \citep{hambro2022dungeons .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Specifically, we recorded the dungeon levels and experience levels achieved in each game, as well as whether the game resulted in an ascension.
elements.sent-2411-332: Utilizing these statistics, we constructed a data-centric progression system where each data point represents the probability of a human player winning the game after reaching a specific dungeon level or experience level.
elements.sent-2411-333: The resulting progression curves are presented in Figure \ref{NLE_progression .}
elements.sent-2411-334: For practical purposes, we define Dungeon Level 1 (Dlvl:1) and Experience Level 1 as representing 0\% progression, corresponding to the game’s starting point, and ascension as 100\% progression.
elements.sent-2411-335: The agent’s overall progress is thus determined by the highest progression achieved between the dungeon level and experience level attained.
elements.sent-2411-336: We provide NetHack results for LLM and VLM mode in Tables~\ref{LLM_NLE and~\ref{VLM_NLE .}}
elements.sent-2411-337: Standard errors are computed using 5 seeds.
elements.sent-2411-338: However, it is still very far from making any significant progression in the game.
elements.sent-2411-339: The best individual run was achieved by Gemini-1.5-Pro vision-language mode, reaching dungeon level 3 and experience level 4.

elements.sent-2411-346: Agents need extensive knowledge to play NetHack effectively.
elements.sent-2411-347: Skilled players leverage their understanding of the game, acquired through hours of gameplay, while new players often rely on written resources online, such as the NetHack Wiki.
elements.sent-2411-348: We use a questionnaire to test if LLMs possess some of this knowledge and assess if agents can apply it.
elements.sent-2411-350: Are there any consequences of eating rotten food in NetHack? (Answer: It might lead to blindness, hallucination or even instant death.).
elements.sent-2411-351: Are there any consequences of eating too much food in NetHack? (Answer: The player character might choke on the food and die.).
elements.sent-2411-352: What happens when you ascend the stairs on level 1 of NetHack? (Answer: It leads to instant game over.).
elements.sent-2411-353: Is it a good idea to use an invisibility wand on a monster in NetHack? (Answer: No, as it will be harder to fight them.).
elements.sent-2411-354: Is it a good idea to pray to an angered god in NetHack? (Answer: No, the god will punish the player.).
elements.sent-2411-355: These questions test a fundamental understanding of the game mechanics, particularly focusing on behaviors that new players may mistakenly attempt and should be avoided.
elements.sent-2411-356: We summarize each LLM's responses in the tables below.
elements.sent-2411-357: For each question, we assess whether the response is accurate, whether the conclusion is correct (i.e., the LLM recognizes the need to avoid such behavior), and whether the agent successively avoids these mistakes.

elements.subsection-2411-021: Analysis of VLM Performance in the Crafter Environment
elements.sent-2411-386: To investigate the agentic capabilities of VLMs in visually complex environments, we focused on the Crafter environment, a simplified 2D version of Minecraft.
elements.sent-2411-387: The environment uses tiled 2D textures to render scenes, which may be out-of-distribution for many VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-388: To address this potential limitation, we introduced an augmented version of the environment where scenes are rendered in 3D using Minecraft's 3D models and textures.
elements.sent-2411-389: This approach leverages data types more likely to be present in the training datasets of VLMs, given the abundance of interleaved text and images about Minecraft on the web.
elements.sent-2411-390: Surprisingly, the switch to 3D rendering did not improve VLM performance.
elements.sent-2411-391: In some cases, performance on the 3D-rendered environment was slightly worse than on the original 2D textures.
elements.sent-2411-392: This result challenges the hypothesis that familiarity with Minecraft-like 3D data in training datasets would lead to better performance.
elements.sent-2411-393: Instead, it suggests that VLMs may struggle with agentic tasks for reasons unrelated to the type of visual texture or rendering style.
elements.sent-2411-394: Our findings indicate that current VLM models may not be well-suited for complex vision-based reasoning tasks, even when provided with familiar visual contexts.
elements.sent-2411-395: The lack of improvement with 3D rendering suggests that factors other than texture familiarity, such as limitations in spatial reasoning or task-specific adaptation, may play a more significant role in their underperformance.
elements.sent-2411-396: While these preliminary results are insightful, further experimentation is necessary to draw stronger conclusions.

elements.sent-2411-400:
The following are the possible actions you can take in the game, followed by a short description of each action: 
ewline
ewline idle: wait for one step,
ewline up: take one step up,
ewline right: take one step to the right,
ewline down: take one step down,
ewline left: take one step to the left.
ewline 
ewline Tips:
ewline - Examine the level carefully, noting all objects and text blocks present.
ewline - Identify the current rules, which are formed by text blocks in the format "[Subject] IS [Property]" (e.g. "BABA IS YOU").
ewline - Consider how you can change or create new rules by moving text blocks around.
ewline - Remember that you can only move objects or text that are not defined as "STOP" or similar immovable properties.
ewline - Your goal is usually to reach an object defined as "WIN", but this can be changed.
ewline - Think creatively about how changing rules can alter the properties and behaviors of objects in unexpected ways.
ewline - If stuck, try breaking apart existing rules or forming completely new ones.
ewline - Sometimes the solution involves making yourself a different object or changing what counts as the win condition.
ewline
